steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The question asks which statement about Alfagali and other Africanist scholars is best supported by the text. The passage explains why a discrepancy exists between historians of capitalism and Africanist scholars: historians of capitalism rely on longitudinal economic data from archival records, which "do not exist for much of precolonial Africa." That means Africanist scholars studying precolonial Africa must use something else.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — They likely view capitalism as having been more beneficial for Africa than historians of capitalism do.: The text says nothing about either group's view of capitalism's benefits. ✗
  • Choice B — They likely have a different view about which activities should be considered capitalist in nature than historians of capitalism do.: The discrepancy is about evidence sources, not definitions of capitalism. ✗
  • Choice C — They likely make use of different types of evidence than historians of capitalism typically rely on.: Directly supported — archival records don't exist for precolonial Africa, so Africanist scholars must rely on different evidence. ✓
  • Choice D — They likely differ from historians of capitalism in the methods they use to derive longitudinal economic data from archival records.: This contradicts the passage; the problem is that archival records don't exist for precolonial Africa, not that the two groups use different methods on the same records. ✗
That is the correct answer.
